commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
रूपं लक्षयति । चक्षुरिति । संख्यादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय मात्रपदम् ।
रूपत्वेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् । नन्वव्याप्यवृत्तिनीलादिसमुदाय एव चित्ररूपमिति
चेत् न । रूपस्य व्याप्यवृत्तित्वनियमात् । ननु चित्रपटे अवयवरूपस्यैव प्रतीतिरिति
चेत् न । रूपरहितत्वेन पटस्याप्रत्यक्षत्वप्रसंगात् । न च रूपवत्समवेतत्वं प्रत्यक्षत्वप्रयोजकं
गौरवात् । तस्मात्पटस्य प्रत्यक्षत्वान्यथानुपपत्त्या चित्ररूपसिद्धिः । रूपस्याश्रयमाह ।
पृथिवीति । आश्रयं विभज्य दर्शयति । तत्रेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines colour: Caksur.... The term only is
added to the definition to avoid the defect of overapplicability
in number etc.[45. The eye can apprehend number too. By inserting only in
the definition, the defect of ativyāpti is avoided as number is recognised
by touch too and not by the eye only] To avoid overapplicability in the generic
attribute of colour (rūpatva) the term special quality is inserted
in the definition. It is admitted that the same organ which
apprehends a quality apprehends its jātī also. The term special
quality is inserted to avoid overapplicability in the contact
of light with wall. Though the contact is a quality, it is
not a special quality.
Objection: Variegated colour is nothing but the mixture of
several colours like blue etc which pervade only a part of
objects. (So, why recognize it as a distinct colour?) [46. What the opponent intends to say is that each of the component
colours in a cloth of variegated colour is non-pervasive in the sense
that it does not characterise the whole of the thing forming its support.
Therefore, since component colours being non-pervasive, the compound
colour is nothing but a collection of these various component colours.
Then, where is the necessity to postulate a distinct colour like the
citra colour?]
Reply: It cannot be so; because, of the invariable law of
colour being a quality that resides in the entire object.[47. The above argument can be sustained only on the assumption
that component colours are non-pervasive entities. Annambhatta refutes
this by affirming that colour, as a rule, is a pervasive entity]
Objection: Instead of admitting of a separate single variegated
colour why not accept that only through the different colours
of its component parts a piece of cloth is apprehended as
having different colours?
Reply: If this argument is accepted, then cloth would be
non-perceptible inasmuch as cloth itself possesses no colour. [48. The view-point of the opponent is that what we see as
the citra colour is really the collection of varied colours of the
different parts of cloth. It is only the various coloured parts that
masquerade as the whole colour of the cloth. Acceptance of this
view-point poses a problem to Annambhatta. If the cloth as a whole
does not possess any colour of its own, then cloth can never become
an object of visual perception; for, a substance without some manifest
colour cannot be visually perceived. But it is perceived; then, which
colour is it? It cannot be the colour of any of its component parts
for the reason mentioned above. Therefore, the colour of the cloth
must be the one which is the total effect of all the colours of
its component parts. This total effect of all the colours is a simple
distinctive colour over and above the colours of its component parts.
And, according to Annambatta, this distinctive colour is known as
citra rūpa]
Objection: If the above argument is accepted, it will lead
to a conclusion that a piece of cloth is not possessing any
colour. A piece of cloth possesses colour but not directly.
There is close contact between cloth and its threads (in the
same sense in which a clay-made-pot is accepted to have
inseparable contact with clay); so, here too, cloth possesses
the colour on account of its being samaveta (inherent or
inseparably united) in its components, ie threads in which
colour exists.
Reply: Accepting such a condition for perceptibility is
cumbersome, and is not preferable to the usual reasoning
of rūpavatva (having colour). Therefore, we must recognise
an independent variegated colour to account for the perceptibility
of a variegated piece of cloth. Non-pervasiveness (avyāpya
vrttitvam) of a thing is the characteristic property of being
existent in the same substratum with its absolute negation.
The Text explains the substratum of colour: Prthivi....
The substratum is again divided by the Text: Tatra....

verses.original_text:
2. Taste (rasa):
रसनग्राह्यो गुणो रसः । स च मधुराम्ललवणकटुकषायतिक्तभेदात्
षड्विधः । पृथिवीजलवृत्तिः । तत्र पृथिव्यां षड्विधः । जले
मधुर एव ॥ २ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
2. Savour is the quality apprehended by the gustatory
organ; it is of six varieties: sweet, acid, saline, pungent,
astringent and bitter. Taste subsists in earth and water.
Of these, earth has all the six varieties while water
has only the sweet variety.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
रसं लक्षयति । रसनेति । रसत्वेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् । रसस्याश्रयमाह ।
पृथिवीति । आश्रयं विभज्य दर्शयति । तत्रेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The text gives the definition of savour: rasanā.... To avoid
the defect of overapplicability in the generic attribute of savour
(rasatva), the word quality is inserted in the definition. The
substratum of savour is shown by the Text: Prthivi.... The
substratum is further explained by its division: pṛthivyām....

verses.original_text:
3. Odour (gandha):
घ्राणग्राह्यो गुणो गन्धः । स द्विविधः
सुरभिरसुरभिश्च । पृथिवीमात्रवृत्तिः ॥ ३ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
3. Odour is the quality perceived by the olfactory
organ. It is twofold; fragrant and foul. It subsists in
earth alone.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: गन्धं लक्षयति । घ्राणेति । गन्धत्वेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines odour: ghrāna.... To avoid the defect
of overapplicability in the generic attribute of odour (gandhatva),
the term quality is inserted in the definition.

verses.original_text:
4. Touch (Sparśa):
त्वगिन्द्रियमात्रग्राह्यो गुणः स्पर्शः । स च त्रिविधः
शीतोष्णानुष्णाशीतभेदात् । पृथिव्यप्तेजोवायुवृत्तिः । तत्र शीतो
जले । उष्णस्तेजसि । अनुष्णाशीतः पृथिवीवाय्वोः ॥ ४ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
4. Touch is the quality perceived by the tactile
organ alone. It is threefold: cool, hot, and lukewarm.
It resides in water, earth, light and air. Of these, cool
touch resides in water, hot touch in light and lukewarm
in earth and air.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
स्पर्श लक्षयति । त्वगिति । स्पर्शत्वेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् । संयोगादावतिव्या-
प्तिवाराणाय मात्रपदम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The definition of touch is given in Text: tvagindriya. To
avoid overapplicability of the definition in the generic attribute
of touch, the word quality is inserted in the definition. Alone
is inserted in the definition to avoid overapplicability in
conjunction.[49. The insertion of alone in the definition is intended to avoid
the defect of ativyapti in contact of the tactile sense with a tactile
substance. The tactile sense is supposed to apprehend only touch,
either warm or cold. According to the Vaiśeşika Ontology, contact
also is a guna; but contact is not the same as warm or cold.
So, insertion of alone removes this difficulty, as contact is apprehended
by the visual organ also]

verses.original_text:
5. The Theory of Pāka:
रूपादिचतुष्टयं पृथिव्यां पाकजमनित्यं च । अन्यत्र अपाकजं
नित्यमनित्यं च । नित्यगतं नित्यम् अनित्यगतमनित्यम् ॥ ५ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
5. The four qualities beginning with colour are
produced in earth through the application of heat, and
are non-eternal. In other substances (ie in water, light
and air) they are not produced by the application of
heat (ie natural) and are eternal and also non-eternal;
in eternals (ie in atoms) they are eternal; and in
non-eternals (ie in products) they are non-eternal.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
पाकजमिति । पाकस्तेजः संयोगः । तेन पूर्वरूपं नश्यति रूपान्तरमुत्पद्यत इत्यर्थः ।
तत्र परमाणुष्वेव पाको न द्वयणुकादौ । आमनिक्षिप्ते घटे परमाणुषु रूपान्तरोत्पत्तौ
श्यामघटनाशे पुनद्वर्यणुकादिक्रमेण रक्तघटोत्पत्तिः । तत्र परमाणवः समवायिकारणम् ।
तेजः संयोगोऽसमवायिकारणम् । अदृष्टादिकं निमित्तकारणम् ।
द्वचणुकादिरूपे
कारणरूपमसमवायिकारणमिति पीलुपाकवादिनो वैशेषिकाः । पूर्वघटस्य नाशं विनैव
अवयविनि अवयवेषु च परमाणुपर्यन्तेषु युगपद्रूपान्तरोत्पत्तिरिति पिठरपाकवादिनो
तैयायिकाः । अत एव पार्थिवपरमाणुरूपादिकमनित्यमित्यर्थः । अन्यत्र जलादावित्यर्थः ।
नित्यगतमिति । परमाणुगतमित्यर्थः । अनित्यगतमिति । द्वयणुकादिगतभित्यर्थः ।
रूपादिचतुष्टयम् उद्भूतं प्रत्यक्षम् । अनुद्भूतमप्रत्यक्षम् । उद्भूतत्वं प्रत्यक्षत्वप्रयोजको
धर्मः । तदभावोऽनुद्भूतत्वम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
Contact with light is pāka. The meaning is that by the
contact of light with earth the colour (of the earthen product)
existing prior to its contact with light gets destroyed and
an altogether different colour is produced in its place. This
process of baking takes place in atoms only and not in dyads
etc.
When an unbaked jar is baked in mild heat, the jar
of black colour gets disintegrated (down to the stage of atoms
along with the qualities in those atoms); and a different colour
is produced (in those atoms). Again, by integration of (fresh)
dyads and triads, etc a new jar of red colour comes into
being. Here atoms form the inherent cause (of the new red
colour) and the contact of light forms the non-inherent cause
of (the new red colour of the atoms), and the unseen results
of the actions of the individual soul (adrsta) for whom it
is to be created, constitute the instrumental cause. The Vaiśeşika
theorists known as Pīlupākavādins hold the view that the
effect of colour etc is produced in atoms only and not in
dyads etc. But the Naiyāyikas known as Pitharapākavādins
hold the view that the contact of light destroys only the
black colour of the jar down to the stage of atoms without
destroying the old black jar itself and while producing
simultaneously an altogether different red colour in the
component parts down to the stage of the atoms. This is
why it is said that colour etc subsisting in the atoms of
earth are non-eternal.
By anyatra in the text, other substances like water, light
and air are meant. The word nityagatam indicates (the four
qualities) subsisting in the atoms. By anityagatam it is implied
that (the four qualities) subsisting in dyads etc are non-eternal.
The four qualities beginning with colour possess manifested
perceptibility. Those which do not possess manifest perceptibility
are imperceptible. Manifest perceptibility is the attribute which
helps in direct perception and the lack of this attribute is
Non-perceptibility.
[50. According to the Vaisesikas, the change that takes place due
to application of heat (pakaja change) is affected directly in the
ultimate (ie atomic) constituents of gross earth-substance. He gives
an example of an unbaked jar. When it is subjected to some degree
of heat, its colour changes from black to red under the influence
of heat. This change we see in the gross jar is because of the
change that undergoes directly in the atoms which are the ultimate
constituents of the gross object. Tejas-particles induce motion in earth
paramāņus; this motion effects the separation of atoms in a dvyanuka.
With the disintegration of dvyanuka, tryanuka also disintegrates, which
finally leads to the disintegration of the gross jar itself which is
the ultimate effect in this causal-series. Thus, it is the destruction
of contact of paramāņu with Tejas particles that finally results in
the destruction of the jar this is known as pākaja-effect induced
by pāka-kriyā. As the Paramāņu separates from dvyanuka, its former
black colour gets destroyed and in its place appears red colour under
the influence of contact with Tejas. These paramāņus with their changed
colour are the starting point of another series of causal changes
which finally culminates in the emergence of a red jar. Here, the
change is only in the quality of atoms and not in the substance
as such because a paramāņu is indivisible and changeless. This change
of quality is a positive effect (bhāva kārya) and has its samavāyi
kāraṇa in the paramāņu as a dravya or substance; its asamavāyi
kāraṇa is in its contact with Tejas particles or pāka. This contact
inheres in a paramāņu. The nimitta karana or the instrumental cause,
is in the Adrsta of the jīva for whom the jar is being produced.
The change of colour in a paramāņu is due to paka consisting
in asamavāyi kāraṇa. The red colour in a dvyanuka is due to the
red colour of its constituent atoms and the samavāyi kāraṇa of the
red colour of a dvyanuka is the dvyanuka itself and the nimitta
karana is the adrsta that was operative in the case of the change
of quality in the paramāņu.
While the Naiyāyikas accept the Vaisesika theory that the four
specific qualities of earth substance (viz rūpa, rasa, gandha and sparśa)
are affected by the application of heat (paka kriyā), they differ from
the Vaiśeşikas with regard to the process of change in colour from
black to red. According to the latter, pāka kriyā (ie change in colour
due to heat) takes place only in earth paramāņus. But the former
hold that like the atoms, the jar too undergoes a similar change
at the same time]

verses.original_text:
6. Number-Sankhyā:
एकत्वादिव्यवहारहेतुः संख्या । सा नवद्रव्यवृत्तिः
एकत्वादिपरार्धपर्यन्ता । एकत्वं नित्यमनित्यं च । नित्यगतं
नित्यम् । अनित्यगतमनित्यम् । द्वित्वादिकं तु
सर्वत्राऽनित्यमेव ॥ ६ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
6. Number is the cause of common usage of
(enumerative) expressions like one, two, etc. It is found
in all the nine substances. It starts from one and ends
with parārdha. Oneness is eternal and non-eternal (ie
eternal in atoms and non-eternal in products); eternal
in eternal substances and non-eternal in non-eternals.
Number two and higher numbers are only non-eternals
everywhere.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: संख्यां लक्षयति । एकेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines number: Ekatvādi....
(Parārdha is the highest number equivalent to a lakh of
lakhs of crores, which constitutes half of the life-span of
Brahma, the creator.)

verses.original_text:
7. Dimension (Parimāṇam):
मानव्यवहारासाधारणकारणं
परिमाणम् ।
नवद्रव्यवृत्तिः ।
तच्चतुर्विधम् । अणु महत् दीर्घ ह्रस्वं चेति ॥ ७ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
7. Dimension is the special cause of the common
usage of words pertaining to measurement. It is found
in all the nine substances. And, it is of four varieties:
atomic, large, long, and short.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
परिमाणं लक्षयति । मानेति । परिमाणं विभजते । तच्चेति । भावप्रधानो निर्देशः ।
अणुत्वं, महत्त्वं, दीर्घत्वं, ह्रस्वत्वं चेत्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines dimension: Māna.... Dimension is divided:
taccha.... Aṇu, etc in the Text indicate aṇutva etc that is
to say, minuteness, largeness, length and shortness.
[51. Further, parimāņa is a real feature of a thing and is not
something just fancied about; eg when we say, this thing is big,
we really intend to describe the thing with certain specification with
reference to its size which is not a mere subjective whim but an
objective reality. As Realists, the Vaisesikas term this feature of a
substance as parimāna or dimension. To exclude Iśvara, time, dik
and adrsta which are the common causes of all products, the word
'special' is inserted in the definition. The dimension of atoms known
as pārimandalya - infinitesimality and the dimension of vibhu-dravyas
(all-pervasive substances), are eternal; all the rest are non-eternal]

verses.original_text:
8. Distinctness (Pṛthaktvam):
पृथग्व्यवहारासाधारणकारणं पृथक्त्वम् । सर्वद्रव्यवृत्तिः ॥ ८ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
8. Distinctness is the special cause of conventional
expressions such as this is distinct from that. It is found
in all the nine substances.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: पृथक्त्वं लक्षयति । पृथगिति । इदमस्मात्पृथक् इति व्यवहारकारणं पृथक्त्वमित्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines distinctness: prthak.... That which causes
the expression of distinctness of one object from another
is severalty. 
[52. It has been argued that distinctness can as well be considered
a kind of abhāva (absence) known as anyonyābhāva (ie mutual absence).
Vaiśeşikas do not accept this theory as it would go against their
enumeration of qualities as twenty four; further, the apprehension
this is distinct from that is a positive experience; it being so, to
consider it a mere absence is doing violence to our consciousness;
hence, it is preferable to accept this awareness as a quality rather
than as a kind of absence]

verses.original_text:
9. Conjunction (Samyoga):
संयुक्तव्यवहारहेतुः संयोगः । सर्वद्रव्यवृत्तिः ॥ ९ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
9. Conjunction is the special cause of conventional
expressions of contact between two objects. It exists
in all the substances.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
संयोगं लक्षयति । संयुक्तेति । इमौ संयुक्तौ इति व्यवहारहेतुरित्यर्थः । संख्यादिलक्षणे
सर्वत्र दिक्कालादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय असाधारणेति विशेषणीयम् । संयोगो द्विविधः कर्मजः
संयोगजश्वेति । आद्यो हस्तक्रियया हस्तपुस्तकसंयोगः । द्वितीयो
हस्तपुस्तकसंयोगात्कायपुस्तकसंयोगः । अव्याप्यवृत्तिः संयोगः । स्वात्यन्ताभावसमाना-
धिकरणत्वमव्याप्यवृत्तित्वम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines conjunction: samyukta.... The expression
which causes the idea: these are in conjunction with each other,
is conjunction. Everywhere the term special ought to be given
while denoting the characteristic mark of number etc in order
to remove the defect of overapplicability in space, time, etc.
Conjunction is of two varieties: born of action and of contact.
The former arises when a book comes in contact with hand,
due to the activity of the hand in grasping a book. The
second variety is the contact which arises between one's body
taken as a whole and a book, when the book is in contact
with one's hand. Conjunction is a non-pervasive (quality).
Non-pervasiveness in a thing is the characteristic quality of
being co-existent in the same substratum with its absolute
negation. 
[53. According to the Vaiśeşikas, conjunction (samyoga) is a quality
inhering in a substance. It is an objective reality due to which the
expression signifying conjunction is possible, eg when the book is
on the table. It is again of two kinds, born of action or direct,
and born of contact itself or indirect; eg the contact of a hand
and a book is born of action and is the direct samyoga. Indirect
samyoga is due to samyoga itself, that is to say, the direct contact
of a book with the hand indirectly results in the contact of the
book with the entire body of which the hand is only a part.
Further, according to the Vaiśeşikas, samyoga is a avyāpya vṛtti,
ie non-pervasive property. This non-pervasiveness is when the thing
and its absence have the same substratum; eg when the hand is
in contact with the table, the absence of that contact also is in
the table (taking the entire table as the substratum), or, it also might
be that even when the hand is in contact with the table, there
is the awareness that the hand is not a part of the table. Thus,
avyāpya comes to be defined as being co-existent in the same locus
where there is also the absolute negation]

verses.original_text:
10. Disjunction (Vibhāga):
संयोगनाशको गुणो विभागः । सर्वद्रव्यवृत्तिः ॥ १० ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
10. Disjunction is the quality which destroys
conjunction. It resides in all the nine substances.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
विभागं लक्षयति । संयोगेति । कालादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुण इति ।
रूपादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय संयोगनाशक इति । विभागोऽपि द्विविधः कर्मजो विभागजश्वेति ।
आद्यो हस्तपुस्तकविभागः । द्वितीयो हस्तपुस्तकविभागात्कायपुस्तकविभागः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines disjunction: samyoga.... The word quality
is inserted in the definition to avoid over-applicability in time
etc. To avoid overapplicability in colour etc the term destroyer
of contact is used in the definition. Disjunction is also of
two kinds: born of action and born of disjunction. The first
variety is the disjunction of a book from the hand caused
by the action of the hand (in throwing away the book).
The second variety is the disjunction of the book from the
body caused by the disjunction of the book from the hand.

verses.original_text:
11. Remoteness - Paratva and Proximity (aparatva):
परापरव्यवहारासाधारणकारणे परत्वापरत्वे । पृथिव्यादिचतुष्टय
मनोवृत्तिनी । ते द्विविधे दिक्कृते कालकृते च । दूरस्थे दिक्कृतं
परत्वं । समीपस्थे दिक्कृतमपरत्वम् । ज्येष्ठे कालकृतं परत्वम् ।
कनिष्ठे कालकृतमपरत्वम् ॥ ११ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
11. Remoteness and proximity are the special causes
of common usage of words expressing remoteness and
proximity. They reside in four substances beginning with
earth, and also in mind. Each of them is of two varieties:
spatial and temporal. In things existing at a distance,
spatial remoteness is found; in things near, spatial
proximity is found; in an elderly person, temporal
remoteness is found; in a younger person, temporal
proximity is found.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
परव्यवहारासाधारणकारणं परत्वम् ।
परत्वापरत्वयोर्लक्षणमाह । परापरेति ।
अपरव्यवहारासाधारणकारणमपरत्वम् इत्यर्थः । ते विभजते । ते द्विविधे इति ।
दिक्कृतयोरुदाहरणमाह । दूरस्थइति । कालकृते उदाहरति । ज्येष्ठ इति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines remoteness and proximity: Para.... The
special cause of expression of posteriority is remoteness. The
special cause of expression of priority is proximity. Remoteness
and proximity are divided by the Text: te dvividhe.... The
example for remoteness and proximity caused by spatial distance
and nearness is shown: dūrasthe.... The example for remoteness
and proximity caused by temporal distance and nearness is
shown in the Text: jyesthe....

verses.original_text:
12. Heaviness (Gurutvam):
आद्यपतनासमवायिकारणं गुरुत्वम् । पृथिवीजलवृत्ति ॥ १२ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
12. Heaviness is the non-intimate cause of the first
down-ward motion in a falling substance. It resides
in earth and water.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
गुरुत्वं लक्षयति । आद्येति । द्वितीयादिपतनस्य वेगासमवायिकारणत्वाद्वेगेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय
आद्येति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines Heaviness: ādya.... Velocity is the
non-intimate cause of the second and subsequent acts of
falling of a substance. Therefore, to avoid overapplicability
(of the definition) in velocity, the word ādya is used in
the definition.

verses.original_text:
13. Fluidity - (dravatvam):
आद्यस्यन्दनासमवायिकारणं द्रवत्वं । पृथिव्यप्तेजोवृत्ति ।
त‌द्विविधं, सांसिद्धिकं नैमित्तिकं च । सांसिद्धिकं जले ।
नैमित्तिकं पृथिवीतेजसोः । पृथिव्यां घृतादावग्नि संयोगजं
द्रवत्वम् । तेजसि सुवर्णादौ ॥ १३ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
13. Fluidity is the non-intimate cause of the first
flow (of a liquid substance). It resides in earth, water
and light. It is of two varieties: natural and artificial.
In water, natural fluidity is found. In earth and light,
artificial fluidity is found. In earth artificial fluidity is
generated through the contact of fire with ghee. Artificial
fluidity is found in gold etc belonging to light.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
द्रवत्वं लक्षयति । आद्येति । स्यन्दनं प्रस्रवणम् । तेजःसंयोगजं नैमित्तिकम् । तद्भिन्नं
सांसिद्धिकम् । पृथिव्यां नैमित्तिकमुदाहरति । घृतादाविति । तेजसि तदाह ।
सुवर्णादाविति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines fluidity: ādyasyandana.... Flow is the
act of flowing of a liquid substance. The fluidity generated
through the contact of light is artificial fluidity. The one other
than this is the natural fluidity. An example of artificial fluidity
in earth is given in ghee coming in contact with fire. In
the same way, artificial fluidity in light is exemplified in gold.
(The word adya is inserted in the definition to exclude velocity
which is the non-intimate cause of the second and all subsequent
acts of flowing).

verses.original_text:
14. Viscidity (Snehah):
चूर्णादिपिण्डीभावहेतुर्गुणः स्नेहः । जलमात्रवृत्तिः ॥ १४ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
14. Viscidity is the quality which causes the
agglutination of powder etc. It exists in water only.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
स्नेहं लक्षयति । चूर्णेति । कालादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् । रूपादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय
पिण्डीभावेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines viscidity: curna.... The word quality
is inserted in the definition to exclude time etc (which are
the common causes of all effects). To avoid the defect of
over-applicability in colour etc the word Pindībhāva etc is
used in the definition.
[54. Sneha is the special quality which helps solidification by
bringing about a peculiar combination among particles of powder.
To the argument that fluidity is the agglutinating agent and not sneha,
Naiyāyikas answer that if it is so, liquefied gold should be the cause
of agglutination of powder etc. But liquefied gold does not seem
to possess this quality of agglutination. So, the agent that causes
agglutination is sneha]

verses.original_text:
15. Sound (Sabdah):
श्रोत्रग्राह्यो गुणः शब्दः, आकाशमात्रवृत्तिः । स द्विविधः,
ध्वन्यात्मकः वर्णात्मकश्च । तत्र ध्वन्यात्मकः भेर्यादौ । वर्णात्मकः
संस्कृतभाषादिरूपः ॥ १५ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
15. Sound is the quality apprehended by the organ
of hearing. It subsists in ether alone. It is twofold:
of the nature of inarticulate sound and of the nature
of the alphabetic sound. Inarticulate sound is found
in the sound produced by drums etc. The alphabetic
sound is found in the form of languages like Sanskrit
etc.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
शब्दं लक्षयति । श्रोत्रेति । शब्दत्वेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुणपदम् । रूपादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय
श्रोत्रेति । शब्दास्त्रिविधः संयोगजः, विभागजः, शब्दजश्वेति । तत्र आद्यो
भेरीदण्डसंयोगजन्यः । द्वितीयो वंशे पाटचमाने दळद्वयविभागजन्यश्चटचटाशब्दः ।
भेर्यादिदेशमारभ्य श्रोत्रदेशपर्यन्तं द्वितीयादिशब्दाः शब्दजाः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines sound: śrotra.... The word quality is
inserted in the definition to avoid the defect of overapplicability
in soundness.[55. Since soundness too is perceived by the organ of hearing.
Soundness being the generic attribute of sound (jāti), gets excluded
by the insertion of quality in the definition] To avoid the defect of overapplicability in
colour etc the word śrotra is used in the definition. Sound
is of three kinds: born of conjunction; born of disjunction;
and born of sound. The first kind of sound is produced
by the contact of a stick with a drum; the decrepitating
sound that is produced when a bamboo is halved is the
second kind of sound; all subsequent sounds reaching the
ear from the place where the sound is produced first, is
the third kind of sound.
[56. Sound is three-fold according to its source of origination.
The peculiar samyoga between two material bodies (like a stick and
a drum) produces one type of sound. The second variety of sound
is produced due to vibhāga disjunction, eg when a bamboo is
split. The third variety is when a series of sound is produced by
the initial sound. When a drum is hit by a stick, it produces the
first sound by means of samyoga. This first one generates the second
sound which in turn generates the third sound and so on and on
till the sound reaches the far-off auditory organs. This is the third variety of sound]

verses.original_text:
16. Cognition (Jñānam):
सर्वव्यवहारहेतुर्गुणो बुद्धिर्ज्ञानम् । सा द्विविधा
स्मृतिरनुभवश्च ॥ १६ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
16. Cognition is the quality which is the cause
of all kinds of communications; and it is knowledge.
It is two-fold: remembrance and apprehension.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
बुद्धेर्लक्षणमाह । सर्वव्यवहारेति । कालादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय गुण इति ।
रूपादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय सर्वव्यवहार इति । जानामीत्यनुव्यवसायगम्यं ज्ञानमेव लक्षणमिति
भावः । बुद्धिं विभजते । सेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The characteristic mark of cognition is given in the Text:
sarva.... The word quality is inserted in the definition to
avoid overapplicability in time etc. To avoid overapplicability
in colour etc the term sarvavyavahāra... is inserted in the
definition. The characteristic mark of cognition is: the immediate
knowledge which becomes the subject (gamya) of the
consciousness (anuvyavasāya) having the form: I know. 
[57. The word vyavahāra in the definition means apt usage of appropriate words. Vyavasaya jñāna is the simple knowledge of a thing, like, this is a jar. In Anuvyavasaya jñāna, the cognition this is a jar becomes the subject matter of the consciousness I know, ie it is visaya jñāna-visayakam jñānam. According to the Nyaya-Vaiśeşka doctrine, anuvyavasaya jñāna is subsequent to vyavasaya jñāna. According to them, the first cognitive state is not self-luminous, ie it does not become the subject of an internal perception. Perceiving a table is a state of cognition; at that moment there is no precise cognition I see a table. It is only at a subsequent instant that he has the consciousness that he sees a table. This subsequent subjective perception is anuvyavasaya jñāna]

verses.original_text:
17. Cognition is twofold: (i) Remembrance (Smrti) and
(ii) Apprehension: (anubhava):
(i) Remembrance (Smrti):
संस्कारमात्रजन्यं ज्ञानं स्मृतिः ॥ १७ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
17. Remembrance is the knowledge born of mental
impressions alone.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
स्मृतेर्लक्षणमाह । संस्कारेति । भावनाख्यः संस्कारः । संस्कारध्वंसेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय
ज्ञानमिति । घटादिप्रत्यक्षेऽतिव्याप्तिवारणाय संस्कारजन्यमिति ।
अतिव्याप्तिवारणाय मात्रपदम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
प्रत्यभिज्ञायाम्
Remembrance is defined by the Text: samskāra. Mental
impression is reminiscent impression. [58. Mental impression is threefold: vega, bhāvanā, and sthitisthāpaka. Of these, only the bhāvanā variety is to be taken here] The word jñānam is
inserted in the definition to avoid overapplicability in the
destruction of mental impression (samskāradhvamsa).[59. Samskaradhvamsa is also born of samskāra, as without samskāra there can be no samskāra-dhvarmsa. Therefore, there is ativyāpti in the definition samskāramātra janyam. To avoid this, jñānam is added to the definition] The term
born of samskāra is added (to the definition) to avoid
overapplicability in perception of things like jar etc. To avoid
overapplicability in reminiscence (pratyabhijñā), the term alone
is used in the definition. 
[60. Identification of the cognition of a thing or a person with its or his recollection of past impression is pratyabhijñā reminiscence. Recognition thus results when immediate cognition arising from the contact of organ with the object of perception is identical with the recollection of past impressions. For instannce, when we say: verily, this is that man, this refers to the cognition arising from the immediate perception of man consequent on the contact of organ; and that refers to the recollection of past impressions relating to the same man. Thus, by inserting the term alone, the defect of overapplicability in reminiscence is avoided. Remembrance (smrti) is born of mental impression alone whereas pratyabhijñā is born out of perception also]

verses.original_text:
18. Apprehension (anubhava):
तद्भिन्नं ज्ञानमनुभवः । स द्विविधः, यथार्थोऽयथार्थश्च ॥ १८ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
18. Knowledge other than recollection is
apprehension. It is twofold: conformable to the object
and not conformable to the object.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
अनुभवं लक्षयति । तद्भिन्नमिति । स्मृतिभिन्नं ज्ञानमनुभव इत्यर्थः । अनुभवं विभजते ।
स द्विविध इति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
Apprehension is defined by the Text: Tadbhinnam. The
meaning is that knowledge other than recollection is
apprehension. Apprehension is divided by the Text: sa dvividhah.

verses.original_text:
19. Valid apprehension (Yathārtha anubhava):
तद्वति तत्प्रकारकोऽनुभवो यथार्थः । यथा रजते इदं रजतमिति
ज्ञानम् । सैव प्रमेत्युच्यते ॥ १९ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
19. A valid apprehension is that in which an object
is known as possessing attributes which it really possesses
eg the apprehension of silver arising in an object where
there is silverness. This is also known as valid knowledge
of a thing.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
यथार्थानुभवस्य लक्षणमाह । तद्वतीति । ननु घटे घटत्वम् इति प्रमायामव्याप्तिः,
घटत्वे घटाभावादिति चेत् न, यत्र यत्संबन्धोऽस्ति तत्र तत्संबन्धानुभवः इत्यर्थाद्धटत्वे
घटसंबन्धोऽस्तीति नाव्याप्तिः । सैवेति । यथार्थानुभव एव शास्त्रे प्रमेत्युच्यत इत्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The characteristic mark of valid apprehension is explained
by the Text: Tadvati....
Objection: If the above definition is admitted, it results
in partial applicability in true apprehension: potness in a pot;
because, jar does not exist in its genus potness. [61. The idea is this: The above definition of pramā applies to a cognition of jar when we interpret tadvati to mean ghatavati; but this cannot apply to a cognition of ghatatva itself as existing in a ghata, because, ghatatva is not the adhikarana of ghata or any other thing. The expression ghate ghatatvam amounts to saying: ghata vrtti ghatatvam, potness residing in a pot. Vrttitvam or ādheyatā is in potness. Through the link, viz ādheyatā (the quality of residing), pot can be accepted to reside as prakāra in potness if adheyatā were accepted as a sambandha of pot and potness. But ādheyatā as such is not recognised as a sambandha (relation) at all. So, pot cannot reside in potness as a prakāra. Accordingly. ghatavati (ghatatve) ghata prakāraka jñānam does not happen. As such partial applicability in the true apprehension potness in a pot has to be accepted]
Reply: It is not so; because where a certain relation exists,
there also occurs the apprehension of that relation. [62. When tadvati is interpreted to mean tatsambandhavati, pot
becomes the substratum of ghatatvasarmbandha, because ghatasambandha,
exists in ghatatva; SO ghatatva becomes the adhikarana of
ghatasambandha. Thus, tatsambandhavati (ghatasambandhavati ghatatve)
tatprakāraka jñānam (ghataprakāraka jñānam) happens] Therefore
there is no defect of avyāpti in the above definition. This
valid cognition is also known as pramā in the scriptures.

verses.original_text:
20. Erroneous apprehension (ayathārtha anubhava):
तदभाववति तत्प्रकारकोऽनुभवोऽयथार्थः । यथा
शुक्ताविदंरजतमिति ज्ञानम् । सैव अप्रमा इत्युच्यते ॥ २० ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
20. That is non-valid apprehension in which an
object is known as having an attribute which it does
not have in reality. For example, the apprehension of
silver arising in a piece of mother-of-pearl. This indeed
is called the false cognition.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
अयथार्थानुभवं लक्षयति । तदभाववतीति । नन्विदं संयोगीति प्रमायामतिव्याप्तिरिति
चेत् न । यदवच्छेदेन यत्संबन्धाभावस्तदवच्छेदेन तत्संबन्धज्ञानस्य विवक्षितत्वात् ।
संयोगाभावावच्छेदेन संयोगज्ञानस्य भ्रमत्वात्संयोगावच्छेदेन संयोगसंबन्धस्य
सत्त्वान्नातिव्याप्तिः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines erroneous cognition: tadabhāvavati... [63. Apramā anubhava is when a cognition has for its prakāra
its inherent jāti while, in reality, the thing suffers from the absence
of that very prakāra; eg the cognition of a snake. Snake must have
the characteristic of snakeness in it in order to render that cognition
valid. Suppose, what really exists before one is a rope and not
a snake; the snakeness which is the prakāra in the above cognition
is absent in a rope which has for its feature only ropeness. Hence,
the cognition of snake is faulty inasmuch as there is no snakeness
in it; hence it is apramā anubhava]
Objection: The above definition brings in overapplicability
in the apprehension: this is in conjunction.[64. The idea of the objection is this: conjunction possesses the
characteristic of partial pervasiveness. Therefore, the adhikarana has
both conjunction and also the negation of conjunction. For example,
when one says, the monkey is sitting on a tree, one gets the knowledge
that the tree is in conjunction with monkey. But the definition of
apramā also applies to this apprehension; because, here we have
kapi samyogaprakāraka anubhava in an adhikarana where there is
also the absence of kapisamyoga in the lower part of the tree]
Reply: No; it is only in erroneous apprehension that the
knowledge of contact is obtained from a part where there
is the absence of contact and what is implied here by apramā
is the knowledge of contact in a particular space where there
is the absence of contact. But in the above apprehension
(kapisamyogīti jñāne), contact is known to exist in the space
where there is the contact of monkey. So there is no defect
of overapplicability.

verses.original_text:
21. Valid apprehension is fourfold:
यथार्थानुभवः चतुर्विधः प्रत्यक्षानुमित्युपमितिशाब्दभेदात् ॥ २१ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
21. Valid apprehension is fourfold due to the division
into perception, inference, analytical knowledge and
verbal testimony.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: यथार्थानुभवं विभजते । यथार्थेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
Valid apprehension is divided by the Text: Yathārtha....

verses.original_text:
22. The instruments of valid apprehension:
तत् करणमपि चतुर्विधं प्रत्यक्षानुमानोपमानशब्द भेदात् ॥ २२ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
22. Their instruments too are fourfold due to division
into the instrument of perception, instrument of inference,
instrument of comparative knowledge; and the instrument
of verbal testimony.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: प्रसङ्गात्प्रमाकरणं विभजते । तत्करणमपीति । प्रमाकरणमित्यर्थः । प्रमाकरणं प्रमाणमिति प्रमाणसामान्यलक्षणम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
In this context, the means of valid apprehension is also being explained.[65. Having mentioned valid apprehension, there arises the need
to mention the means of valid apprehension. So, the means are
being classified and explained by the Text] That which gives rise to right apprehension is proof; this definition is the general characteristic of proof.

verses.original_text:
23. Definition of Special cause:
असाधारणं कारणं करणम् ॥ २३ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary: 23. The uncommon cause (instrumental or efficient) is the special cause.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: करणलक्षणमाह । असाधारणेति । दिक्कालादावतिव्याप्तिवारणाय असाधारणेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The text defines cause: asādhārana.... The term uncommon is inserted in the definition to avoid the defect of overapplicability in time, space etc. 
[66. There are two types of causes: common cause - sādhārana
kāraņa, and uncommon or special cause - asādhāraņa kārana. That
which is the cause of every effect is the general or common cause,
eg space, time, destiny etc. That which is the specific cause of
an effect is called the special or uncommon cause, eg yarn in the
production of cloth]

verses.original_text:
24. Definition of cause:
कार्यनियतपूर्ववृत्ति कारणम् ॥ २४ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary: 24. That which invariably precedes its effect is its cause.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
कारणलक्षणमाह । कार्येति । पूर्ववृत्ति कारणमित्युक्ते रासभादावतिव्याप्तिः स्यादतो नियतेति । तावन्मात्रे कृते कार्येऽतिव्याप्तिरतः पूर्ववृत्तीति । ननु तन्तुरूपमपि पटं प्रति कारणं स्यादिति चेत् न, अनन्यथासिद्धत्वे सतीति विशेषणात् । अनन्यथासिद्धत्वमन्यथासिद्धिरहितत्वं । अन्यथासिद्धिः त्रिविधा - येन सहैव यस्य
यं प्रति पूर्ववृत्तित्वमवगम्यते तं प्रति तेन तदन्यथासिद्धम् । यथा तन्तुनां तन्तुरूपं
तन्तुत्वं च पटं प्रति । अन्यं प्रति पूर्ववृत्तित्वे ज्ञात एव यस्य यं प्रति
पूर्ववृत्तित्वमवगम्यते तं प्रति तदन्यथासिद्धम् । यथा शब्दं प्रति पूर्ववृत्तित्वे ज्ञात
एव पटं प्रत्याकाशस्य । अन्यत्र क्लृप्तनियतपूर्ववतिर्न एव कार्यसंभवे तत्सहभूतमन्यथासिद्धम् ।
यथा पाकजस्थले गन्धं प्रति रूपप्रागभावस्य । एवञ्च अनन्यथासिद्धनियतपूर्ववृत्तित्वं
कारणत्वम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The text defines the cause; kārya... If cause is defined
merely as antecedent, the defect of overapplicability occurs
in animals like ass etc (in the production of a jar). This
is avoided by the inclusion of the term invariably (as an
ass need not be invariably antecedent in the production of
a jar). Again, the mere definition kāryaniyatam kāraņam, brings
in the defect of overapplicability in the produced effect. [67. That is, when so defined, it means that wherever there is
product, there is product. Thus ativyapti occurs in kārya as niyatatva
is found in effect also.]
To avoid this, the term pūrvavṛtti is inserted in the definition
(ie there can be no antecedent effect before the production
of an effect).
Objection: The colour of the yarn also becomes the cause
of cloth (as both kāryaniyatatva and antecedence are found
in colour).
Reply: It is not so; because the definition of karana is
qualified by the adjunct ananyathāsiddhatve sati which means
provided, the antecedent thing is not connected with dispensable
things. Ananyathāsiddha is that which does not depend for
its invariable existence upon some other antecedent existing
thing. [68. The idea is this: The absence of dispensable antecedent is
anan yatāsiddha. There will not happen the absence of dispensable
antecedents when there is the presence of indispensable cause; for
example, the existence of ether and kapālarūpa etc in the production
of a jar, are the dispensable antecedents. In these anyathāsiddhas,
both invariability and antecedence are found, thus causing the defect
of ativyāpti in the definition of karana. To avoid this, the term
ananyathāsiddha is added to the definition] The absence of dispensable antecedents is
ananyathäisiddha.
Anyathāsiddhatva (the dispensable antecedence in the
production of a thing) is threefold: (a) Things that are connected
to inherent relation (samavāya sambandha) with the cause
and thus become the antecedents to effect through them;
as for instance, the colour of yarn (tanturūpa) and the generic
attribute of yarn (tantutva) which are intimately united with
yarn, are antecedent to its effect, viz, a piece of cloth (pata).
Here both tanturūpa and tantutva are the examples for the
first variety of anyathāsiddha. (b) Things that are antecedent
to cause and therefore become antecedent to effect; as for
instance, ether in the production of a jar. Ether is not directly
perceived and its existence is proved only with reference
to sound of which ether is the inherent cause. Thus, ether
becomes antecedent to jar only after it becomes antecedent
to its effect, sound. Therefore, ether is anyathāsiddha in the
production of a jar. This is an example for the second type.
(c) All other concomitants of cause that are not connected
with it by inherence (samavāya). Since the production of an
effect is determined by its invariable antecedence in some
other place, other antecedents that accompany the product
are considered anyathāsiddha: for instance, when heat is applied,
the antecedent non-existence of colour (rūpa prāgabhāva) is
anyathāsiddha in the production of smell. This is the third
type. Thus a cause is defined as the indispensable invariable
antecedence of an effect. 
[69. A mango, possessing all the qualities except smell, gets the
quality of smell when subjected to heat. Here the causes of the
production of odour in the fruit must be the antecedent non-existence
of smell inasmuch as there cannot be any other antecedent non-existence
except the antecedent non-existence of smell, in view of the maxim
of the Naiyāyikas that without the existence of antecedent non-existence
no effect can be produced; otherwise, we should be able to produce
another jar from the clay of an already existing jar. Another mango
possessing all the four qualities except colour and smell, is subjected
to warming. In the process, the fruit gets both colour and smell.
Here a doubt arises as to which antecedent non-existence is the
cause in the production of smell. In places other than the application
of heat, the antecedent non-existence of smell is determined to be
the cause in the production of smell. Therefore, here also, the antecedent
non-existence of smell is essential in the production of smell. Therefore,
the antecedent non-existence of colour is anyathāsiddha here. This
is the example for the third variety of anyathāsiddhatva]

verses.original_text:
25. Definition of effect:
कार्यं प्रागभावप्रतियोगि ॥ २५ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
25. Effect is the counter-correlative of its own
antecedent non-existence (in its inherent cause).

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: कार्यलक्षणमाह । कार्यमिति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives the characteristic mark of effect: kāryam....
[70. Annambhatta defines effect - kārya as the Pratiyogin or
counter correlative of its own antecedent non-existence. Pratiyogin is
used in the context of either abhāva (absence or non-existence) or
sambandha (relation). In the former, pratiyogin refers to the object
whose absence is intended to be established. For ghalābhāva, the
ghata is the pratiyogin of its abhāva. For the abhava of ghatābhāva,
ghatābhāva is the pratiyogin. Effects like a pot, a table, etc come
into being at a certain point of time which proves its antecedent
non-existence as otherwise expressions such as 'will come into existence'
would be redundant. So, before the effect comes into existence,
there was its absence; this is known as antecedent non-existence
or prāgabhāva and an effect like a ghata is the pratiyogin of such
antecedent non-existence]

verses.original_text:
26. Cause (Kāranam):
कारणं त्रिविधम् - समवाय्यसमवायिनिमित्तभेदात् ॥ २६ ॥
27. The inherent cause (samavāyi kāraṇam):
यत् समवेतं कार्यं उत्पद्यते तत् समवायिकारणम् ।
यथा तंतवः पटस्य, पटश्च स्वगतरूपादेः ॥ २७ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
26. Cause is of three kinds: inherent, non-inherent,
and occasioning (instrumental or efficient).
[71. Nyāya-Vaiśeşika School recognises that every positive effect
has three causes, viz, samavāya, asamavāya, and nimitta; eg in a
product like cloth, yarn is the inherent cause; the contact of two
yarns is the non-inherent cause; and causes other than these two,
like the weaver, shuttle etc are the efficient causes. Inherent cause
is always a substance; non-inherent cause must be either a quality
or an action and nothing else; while nimitta kāraņa may be of any
kind. Abhāva has only nimitta kāraņa]
27. The inherent cause is that which is in inseparable
union in (the thing in) which the effect is produced,
as the yarns are of the (effect) cloth and the cloth
is of the colour on it.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
कारणं विभजते । कारणमिति । समवायिकारणस्य लक्षणमाह — यत्समवेतमिति ।
यस्मिन् समवेतमित्यर्थः । असमवायिकारणं लक्षयति — कार्येणेति । कार्येणेत्येतदुदाहरति
— तन्तुसंयोग इति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines inherent cause: Yat samavetam.... The
idea is that it is in inseparable union with the effect. 
[72. Samavāyi kāraņa is defined as that inhering in which an
effect emerges, eg the yarn which constitutes the intimate cause of
its effect cloth, because the cloth is connected with the yarns by
inherent union. Similarly, the colour on the piece of cloth being
a quality, resides in the cloth by intimate relation. Therefore, the
piece of cloth is the inherent cause of the colour on it]

verses.original_text:
28. The non-inherent cause (asamavāyi kāraṇam):
कार्येण कारणेन वा सह एकस्मिन्नर्थे समवेतत्वे सति यत्कारणं
तदसमवायिकारणम् । यथा तन्तुसंयोगः पटस्य, तन्तुरूपं
पटरूपस्य ॥ २८ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
28. The non-inherent cause is one which is
inseparably united in the same object either with the
effect, or with the cause, as the conjunction of yarns
in relation to the (effect) cloth or the colour of the
yarns in relation to (the effect) the colour of the cloth.
[73. Here taritusamyoga inheres in yarn with cloth which is the
effect of the contact of yarns. Thus, both the cloth and the tantusamyoga
exist in the same substratum, ie tantus. Therefore, tantusamyoga is
the non-inherent cause to cloth]

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
कार्येण पटेन सह एकस्मिंस्तन्तौ समवेतत्वात्तन्तुसंयोगः पटस्यासमवायिकारणमित्यर्थः ।
कारणेनेत्येतदुदाहरति - तन्तुरूपमिति । कारणेन पटेन सह एकस्मिंस्तंन्तौ समवेतत्वात्

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives two definitions of non-intimate cause:
the first is: kāryeņa saha ekasminnarthe samavetatve sati yat
kāraņam tat asamavāyikāraņam that is the non-inherent cause,
which is inherent in the same substratum along with the
effect. The Text provides the example of the relation of contact
of yarn with cloth to explain this type of non-inherent cause.
The second variety is: kāraṇena saha ekasminnarthe
samavetatve sati yat kāraņam tat asamavāyikāraņam that is
the non-inherent cause which is inherent in the substratum
along with cloth which is the inherent cause of the colour
of cloth (patarūpa).
[74. The example cited here is the relationship of tanturūpa to
patarūpa. Here the colour of yarns inheres in yarns along with cloth
which is the inherent cause of patarūpa, that is to say, the colour
of yarns produces the colour of cloth in cloth inhering in yarn along
with cloth. Therefore, the colour of yarn is the non-inherent cause
to the colour of cloth]

verses.original_text:
29. Occasioning cause (nimitta kāraṇam):
तदुभयभिन्नं कारणं निमित्तकारणम् ।
यथा तुरीवेमादिकं पटस्य ॥ २९ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
29. The cause which is different from both these
causes is the occasioning cause (instrumental or efficient),
such as the shuttle, loom etc in the case of (production
of) a piece of cloth.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
तन्तुरूपं पटरूपस्यासमवायिकारणमित्यर्थः । निमित्तकारणं लक्षयति तदुभयेति ।
समवाय्यसमवायिभिन्नकारणं निमित्तकारणमित्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines the occasioning cause: tadubhaya.... The
cause different from both the intimate and non-intimate causes
is the occasioning cause (either instrumental or efficient).
[75. A cause which is other than either the samavāyi or the
asamavāyi kāraṇa, is the nimitta kāraṇa, eg the activities of the
weaver, the shuttle, the loom etc]

verses.original_text:
30. Special Cause (asādharanam kārannam):
तदेत‌त्त्रिविधकारणमध्ये यदसाधारणं कारणं तदेव
करणम् ॥ ३० ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
30. That alone which is the uncommon cause among
the three varieties of causes mentioned above, is the
special cause.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: करणलक्षणमुपसंहरति तदेतदिति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text concludes the definition of special cause:
Tadetat.... 
[76. Kāraṇa is defined by Annambhatta as an uncommon or special
cause. Keśavamiśra in his Tarkabhāṣā defines karaṇam as sādhakatamam,
ie the most immediate cause in bringing about an effect, that is
to say, it is the most efficient cause. That which directly and immediately
brings about the result by its own action is karaṇam, eg an arrow
in killing a bird. Here, arrow is the special cause which brings about
the instant death of a bird, because, it alone immediately brings
about the result killing the bird by its own activity of striking the
bird dead. Thus karaṇa is a cause which is most essential in producing
a result and without the activity of which the effect is not produced
in spite of the other causes being present. This is the view held
by the modern Naiyāyikas. But the ancient Naiyāyikas define it as
vyāpāravat asādhāraṇam kāraṇam karaṇam ie karaṇa is a special and
operative cause. Vyāpāra is an operation which, not being a substance,
is the product of a thing, and produces the effect of that thing.
The example for this is as follows: The sense organ is karaṇa.
The contact of a sense-organ with its corresponding object is the
vyāpāra. The percept is the effect. The contact of sense-organs happens
to be both cause and effect. It becomes cause with reference to
the effect of organ and becomes also effect with reference to sense
organ. That which is known as vyāpāra by the ancient Naiyāyikas
is known as karaṇa by the modern Naiyāyikas]

verses.original_text:
SECTION IV
THE NYAYA-VAISESIKA
THEORY OF EPISTEMOLOGY
31. Perception (Pratyaksa Pramāņa):
तत्र प्रत्यक्षज्ञानकरणं प्रत्यक्षम् ॥ ३१ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
31. Among them, perception is the special cause
of percept.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
प्रत्यक्षलक्षणमाह
तत्रेति । प्रमाणचतुष्टयमध्ये इत्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines percept: tatra.... Tatra means, among
the four means of apprehension.

verses.original_text:
32. Perception defined and explained:
इन्द्रियार्थसन्निकर्षजन्यं ज्ञानं प्रत्यक्षम् । त‌द्विविधं निर्विकल्पकं
सविकल्पकं चेति । तत्र निष्प्रकारकं ज्ञानं निर्विकल्पकं यथेदं
किश्चित् । सप्रकारकं ज्ञानं सविकल्पकं यथा डित्थोऽयं,
ब्राह्मणोऽयं, श्यामोऽयं, पाचकोऽयमिति ॥ ३२ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
32. Perception is the apprehension born of the
contact of the organ with an object. It is of two kinds:
indeterminate and determinate. Of these, the knowledge
of a thing without any attribute is known as the
indeterminate apprehension; such as, this is something.
Attributive knowledge is determinative apprehension.
Apprehensions like, he is a Dittha, he is a Brahmaņa,
he is black, he is a cook, are examples of determinate
apprehension.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
प्रत्यक्षज्ञानस्य लक्षणमाह — इन्द्रियेति । इन्द्रियं चक्षुरादिकम्, अर्थो घटादिः,
तयोः संनिकर्षः संयोगादिः, तज्जन्यं ज्ञानमित्यर्थः । तद्विभजते । तद्विविधमिति ।
निर्विकल्पकस्य लक्षणमाह — निष्प्रकारकमिति । विशेषणविशेष्यसंबन्धानवगाहि
ज्ञानमित्यर्थः । ननु निर्विकल्पके किं प्रमाणमिति चेत् न, गौरिति विशिष्टज्ञानं
विशेषणज्ञानजन्यं, विशिष्टज्ञानत्वात्, दण्डीति ज्ञानवत् इत्यनुमानस्य प्रमाणत्वात् ।
विशेषणज्ञानस्यापि सविकल्पकत्वेऽनवस्थाप्रसङ्गान्निर्विकल्पकसिद्धिः । सविकल्पकं
लक्षयति । सप्रकारकमिति । नामजात्यादिविशेष्यविशेषणसम्बन्धावगाहि ज्ञानमित्यर्थः ।
सविकल्पकमुदाहरति । यथेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The distinguishing characteristic mark of perception is given
in the Text: Indriya.... The organs are the eye etc. Objects
are jar etc. Their connection is known as contact. That which
is produced as a result of this contact (of organ with the
object) is perception. It is again divided by the Text: it is
of two kinds. The Text then gives the characteristic mark
of indeterminate apprehension. The knowledge in which there
is the absence (anavagahi) of a subject, attribute, and relation
(between the attribute and the subject) is the indeterminate
knowledge.
Question: What is the proof to determine the indeterminate
knowledge?
Reply: The attributive knowledge: this is a cow, is born
of the knowledge of the qualifying attribute cowness; because,
it is an attributive knowledge (viśistajñāna) like the knowledge,
A man with a stick. Inference is thus the proof (in establishing
the indeterminate knowledge). If we say that the knowledge
of the attribute also is determinate knowledge, it will then
and us in the logical anomaly of infinitum regressus. Therefore,
the knowledge of the attribute proved by inference, has to
be accepted to determine the indeterminate knowledge. The
knowledge which comprehends the relation of the qualified
and the qualifier such as name, class etc is known as the
determinate knowledge. Determinate knowledge is exemplified:
yathā.... The Text defines determinate knowledge:
Saprakārakam....
[77. The four examples given in the text are in illustration of
samjñā, name; jāti class; guṇa quality; and kriyā - action]

verses.original_text:
33. The operative cause for perception is six-fold:
प्रत्यक्षज्ञानहेतुरिन्द्रियार्थसंनिकर्षः षड्विधः । संयोगः, संयुक्तसम-
वायः, संयुक्तसमवेतसमवायः, समवायः, समवेतसमवायः,
विशेषणविशेष्यभावश्चेति ॥ ३३ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
33. The conjunction of organs with objects which
causes perception is of six kinds: (a) conjunction; (b)
inherent union with what has come into contact; (c)
inherent union with what is intimately united with a
thing which has come in contact; (d) inherent union;
(e) inherent union with the intimately united; and (f)
the connection of the attribute with the substantive.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: इन्द्रियार्थसंनिकर्ष विभजते । प्रत्यक्षेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text divides conjunction of organ with object:
pratyaksa....

verses.original_text:
34. The first operative cause (Samyoga - sannikarsah):
चक्षुषा घटप्रत्यक्षजनने संयोगः सन्निकर्षः ॥ ३४ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
34. Conjunction is the contact, producing perception
of the jar by the eye.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
संयोगसंनिकर्षमुदाहरति । चक्षुषेति । द्रव्यप्रत्यक्षे सर्वत्र संयोगः संनिकर्षः । आत्मा
मनसा संयुज्यते, मन इन्द्रियेण, इन्द्रियमर्थन, ततः प्रत्यक्षज्ञानमुत्पद्यते इत्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text exemplifies conjunctive contact: cakṣuṣā....
Conjunctive contact is the cause in producing perception of
substances in all cases. The self (ātman) comes in contact
with mind (manas), the mind with the organ (indriya), and
organ with the object (visaya), and then perceptual knowledge
takes place. 
[78. According to the Nyāya doctrine, direct perception is always
dependent on some relation between an Indriya (sense organ) and
an artha (object). This relation is the operative cause in bringing
about a cognition. The cognition may either be laukika (ordinary)
perception of bhāva padārthas (positive entities) like that of a dravya,
guṇa, karma or jāti. Again, we may have perception of a quality
like śabda in ākāśa which is not perceived by the senses; there
occurs also the perception of the universal soundness abiding in sound.
We may have also perception of negative entities like, the absence
of a pot or a table etc. In each case what is necessary is a certain
kind of relation between the artha (object) perceived and the respective
indriya (sense organ) involved in the act of perception. This perception
is again twofold: Savikalpaka (determinant) and Nirvikalpaka
(indeterminant) which has been dealt with earlier]

verses.original_text:
35. Second operative cause (Samyukta samavāya):
घटरूपप्रत्यक्षजनने संयुक्त समवायः संनिकर्षः ।
चक्षुः संयुक्ते घटे रूपस्य समवायात् ॥ ३५ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
35. The inherent union with the conjoint is the
contact in producing the perception of the colour of
a jar, as the colour is inherently united with the jar
which is in contact with the eye.
[79. The colour is intimately united with the jar which is conjointed
with the organ. Hence the contact of the eye with the colour of
the jar is samyukta samavāya]

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: संयुक्तसमवायमुदाहरति । घटरूपेति । तत्र युक्तिमाह । चक्षुःसंयुक्त इति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
Inherent union with the conjoint is exemplified: ghațarūpa....
The process is explained: cakșuh samyukte.

verses.original_text:
36. Third operative cause (Samyukta-samaveta-samavāya):
रूपत्वसामान्यप्रत्यक्षे संयुक्तसमवेतसमवायः सन्निकर्षः । चक्षुः
संयुक्ते घटे रूपं समवेतं, तत्र रूपत्वस्य समवायात् ॥ ३६ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
36. Inherent union with the intimately united is
the contact in producing the perception of the universal
genus colourness, as colour is inherently united with
the jar that is conjoint with the ocular organ, and
the genus colourness is inherently united therewith.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: संयुक्तसमवेतसमवायमुदाहरति । रूपत्वेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives an example of inherent union with the
intimately united - rūpatva....
[80. Here, eye is in contact with the jar with which is intimately
united the colour of the jar in which inheres its genus. Ghatarūpatva
is, therefore, perceived by the eye by means of the contact
samyukta-samavetasamavāya]

verses.original_text:
37. Fourth operative cause (Samavāyaḥ sannikarsah):
श्रोत्रेण शब्दसाक्षात्कारे समवायः सन्निकर्षः कर्णविवरवर्त्याकाशस्य
श्रोत्रत्वात् शब्दस्याकाशगुणत्वात् गुणगुणिनोश्च समवायात् ॥ ३७ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
37. Inherent union is the contact in the perception
of word by the organ of hearing, as the organ of
hearing is the ether in the cavity of the ear inasmuch
as sound is the quality of ether and the quality and
the qualified are inherently united.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
समवायमुदाहरति । श्रोत्रेणेति । तदुपपादयति । कर्णेति । ननु दूरस्थशब्दस्य कथं
श्रोत्रसम्बन्ध इति चेत् न, वीचीतरङ्गन्यायेन कदम्बमुकुलन्यायेन वा शब्दान्तरोत्पत्तिक्रमेण
श्रोत्रदेशे जातस्य श्रोत्रेण संबन्धात्प्रत्यक्षसंभवः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives an example of inherent union: śrotrena.
That is being elaborated Karna....
Question: How does a distant sound come in contact with
the auditory sense?
Reply: Just like the principle of vīcītaranga or
kadambamukula; the first sound produces another sound which
in turn propels another, and so on, till the last sound reaches
the auditory sense, thus enabling one to perceive sound directly. 
[81. Vīcītaranga nyāya is the principle of a wave urging forward
another wave. In the ocean one wave propels another, till the first
and all the subsequent waves reach the shore. In the same way,
there is a successive operation in the case of the production of
sound till they reach the auditory orifice. Kadambamukula nyāya indicates
the simultaneous blossoming forth of the buds in a kadamba tree
when a cloud thunders in the sky]

verses.original_text:
38. Fifth operative cause - Samaveta-samavāya-sannikarsah:
शब्दत्वसाक्षात्कारे समवेतसमवायः सन्निकर्षः श्रोत्रसमवेते शब्दे
शब्दत्वस्य समवायात् ॥ ३८ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
38. Inherent union with the inherently united is
the contact in cognising soundness, as the genus soundness
is inherently united with sound which is inherently united
with the auditory sense.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: समवेतसमवायमुदाहरति । शब्दत्वेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives an example of inherent union with the
intimately united: śabdatva....

verses.original_text:
39. Sixth operative cause (Viśeṣana
Sannikarsa):
Viśeşyabhāva
अभावप्रत्यक्षे विशेषणविशेष्यभावः सन्निकर्षः, घटाभाव-
वद्भूतलमित्यत्र चक्षुः संयुक्ते भूतले घटाभावस्य विशेषणत्वात् ॥ ३९ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
39. The connection of the qualifier and the qualified
is the conjunction in the perception of negation, as
in the cognition: This spot of earth is with the negation
of jar. Here, the negation of a jar is an attribute
of a place in contact with the eye.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
विशेषणविशेष्यभावमुदाहरति । अभावेति । तदुपपादयति । घटाभाववदिति । भूतले
घटो नास्तीत्यत्र अभावस्य विशेष्यत्वं द्रष्टव्यम् । एतेन अनुपलब्धेः प्रमाणान्तरत्वं
निरस्तम् । यद्यत्र घटोऽभविष्यत्तर्हि भूतलमिवाद्रक्ष्यत, दर्शनाभावान्नास्तीति
तर्कितप्रतियोगिसत्त्वविरोध्यनुपलब्धिसहकृतेन्द्रियेणैव अभावज्ञानोत्पत्तौ अनुपलब्धेः
प्रमाणान्तरत्वासंभवात् । अधिकरणज्ञानार्थमपेक्षणीयेन्द्रियस्यैव करणत्वोपपत्तावनुपलब्धेः
करणत्वायोगात् । विशेषणविशेष्यभावो विशेषणविशेष्यस्वरूपमेव, नातिरिक्तः संबन्धः ।
प्रत्यक्षज्ञानमुपसंहरंस्तस्य करणमाह । एवमिति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives an example of the connection of the
attribute and the subject: abhāvah.... This is being explained:
Ghatābhāvavat.... In the cognition this spot of earth is with
the negation of jar, the spot of earth is the qualified and the
negation of jar is the attribute. [82. In this cognition, the eye is in contact with the spot of
earth of which the negation of jar is the attribute, and, therefore,
here, the connection is, contact with the attribute] In the cognition, there is
no jar on the spot of earth, (where the eye is in contact
with the spot of earth of which) the negation of jar is the
qualified. [83. Hence, here, the connection is, contact with the qualified.
Both these cognitions though differing in form, are identical in meaning.
They are conjointly expressed in the Text as viśeṣaṇaviśeşya bhāva.
With the contact of the eye with the spot of earth in the above
two cognitions, the negation either on account of its possessing the
property of Viśeşya or of Viśeṣaṇa becomes perceived on the spot
of earth. The relation between the spot of earth and the negation
of pot is no other than Visesana-Viśeşya bhāva] By this, the necessity for accepting non-perception
as a distinct source of knowledge (anupalabdheḥ pramānāntarat-
vam) is refuted.
If there had been a jar, then the jar would have been
apprehended as existing on that spot of earth. It is due to
its absence, the apprehension, jar is not here, arises. Here,
in this argument, is found the counter-entity of the negation
of apprehension, thus proving that non-perception is only an
accessory (sahakarn) of the eye which alone perceives the
negation of jar on earth. Therefore (in this knowledge of
the negation of jar on earth), the eye alone being the cause
of the knowledge of substratum of negation it is impossible
to state that anupalabdhi (non-perception) is an independent
source to gain the knowledge of negation of jar.[84. The Nyaya School of thought does not accept the necessity
of postulating a distinct source of valid knowledge (pramāṇa) to cognise
absence (abhāva) as this could as well be achieved by perception
in which the operative causal relation is between the viśeṣaṇa and
viśeşya. The absence is apprehended on the same locus (adhikaraṇa).
And this perception of abhāva of a certain thing on a certain locus,
is not to be taken to be merely due to the defect of the eye
or due to insufficient light; but it ought to be preceded by Tarka,
that is to say, by the thought like the following: if a pot were on
the ground, I should be seeing it right now, but I do not see the
pot. This Tarka brings in the awareness of the absence of the pot
on the ground. It is in order to emphasize such effective perception
of absence that Annambhatta mentions in his Dīpikā about the existence
of the pratiyogi (counter entity) of the negation of apprehension.
This further proves that non-perception is necessary for the immediate
apprehension of abhāva; here, non-apprehension is an auxiliary condition
(sahakari) of the eye which alone perceives the ghatābhāva on a
spot of earth. Therefore, admission of a separate pramāņa like
anupalabdhi is redundant when the same can be had by pratyaksa
— this is the Naiyāyika stand-point]
The relationship between the qualifier and the qualified
is nothing but the nature of the attribute and the qualified.
By this no other relationship is meant.

verses.original_text:
40. Conclusion:
एवं सन्निकर्षषट्कजन्यं ज्ञानं प्रत्यक्षं, तत्करणमिन्द्रियम्,
तस्मादिन्द्रियं प्रत्यक्षप्रमाणमिति सिद्धम् ॥ ४० ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
40. Perception is thus the cognition caused by the
sixfold contact, and the organ is its cause (efficient
or instrumental). Therefore, organ is the proof of
perception.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
असाधारणकारणत्वादिन्द्रियं प्रत्यक्षज्ञानकरणमित्यर्थः ।प्रत्यक्षप्रमाणमुपसंहरति ।
तस्मादिति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
Concluding the section dealing with perceptual knowledge,
the Text mentions its special cause: evam.... Since the organ
is the special cause of perception, it is called the efficient
or instrumental cause of perception. He concludes the section
dealing with pratyakṣa pramāṇa by the words: tasmāt....

verses.original_text:
SECTION V
INFERENCE
1. Inference (anumānam):
अनुमितिकरणमनुमानम् ॥ १ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
1. Inference is the special cause of inferential
knowledge.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary: अनुमानं लक्षयति-अनुमितिकरणमिति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text defines inference: anumitikaranam.
[85. Inference is treated after direct perception inasmuch as the
former depends on the latter]

verses.original_text:
2. Inferential knowledge (anumiti):
परामर्शजन्यं ज्ञानमनुमितिः ॥ २ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
2. Inferential knowledge is the knowledge born of
subsumptive reflection.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
अनुमितिं लक्षयति -- परामर्शेति । ननु संशयोत्तरप्रत्यक्षेऽतिव्याप्तिः स्थाणुपुरुषसंशयानन्तरं
पुरुषत्वव्याप्यकरादिमानयम् इति परामर्शे सति पुरुष एव इति प्रत्यक्षजननात् ।
न च तत्रानुमितिरेवेति वाच्यम् । पुरुषं साक्षात्करोमि इत्यनुव्यवसायविरोधादिति
चेत् न, पक्षतासहकृतपरामर्शजन्यत्वस्य विवक्षितत्वात् । सिषाधयिषाविरहविशिष्टसिद्धयभावः
पक्षता । साध्यसिद्धिरनुमितिप्रतिबन्धिका । सिद्धिसत्त्वेऽपि अनुमिनुयाम्
इतीच्छायामनुमितिदर्शनात् सिषाधयिषोत्तेजिका । ततश्चोत्तेजकाभावविशिष्टमण्यभावस्य
दाहकारणत्ववत्सिषाधयिषाविरहविशिष्टसिद्धयभावस्याप्यनुमितिकारणत्वम् ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Text gives the characteristic mark of inferential
knowledge: parāmarśa....
Objection: There is overapplicability in perception in the
form of a conclusion arrived at after first doubting, as in
the example: Is this a post or a man? (On the perception
of an object from a distance in insufficient light, the doubt,
'is this a post or a man?' arises). Then he reasons with
the help of subsumptive reflection: But this object has hands
etc which are found invariably associated with mankind. Then
arises the perception: this indeed is a man. It should not
be argued that the above cognition this indeed is a man
is to be construed as inferential knowledge inasmuch as it
has been derived consequent on parāmarśa, viz, this has hands
etc which is vyāpya (less pervasive) with regard to mankind,
and as it is contrary to anuvyavasaya, like: I am perceiving
a man. 
[86. By raising the above objection the opponent means to say
that in samsayottara pratyaksa a kind of intermediary parāmarśa operates
inasmuch as perceptual experience arises following consideration of
certain particulars of the object, thus causing the defect of ativyāpti
in samsayottara pratyaksa. This defect in the definition is avoided
by prefixing parāmarsa with the adjunct, pakṣatāsahakṛta]
Reply: Not so; because, the defect in the definition is
avoided by prefixing parāmarśa with the adjunct: in association
with pakṣatā. Then it ought to be interpreted as follows:
inferential cognition is the knowledge that arises from parāmarśa
in association with paksatā. Paksatā (ie the characteristic of
an instance) consists in having in it the negation of the
ascertainment of the thing to be inferred, accompanied by a
negation of any desire to infer an already proven thing [87. In samśayottara pratyaksa, there is no paksatājanyatva, because,
man and his hands etc are perceived simultaneously, and as such,
no sādhya is left to be ascertained, and also there could be no
desire to infer. Thus the defect of ativyāpti is removed. Again, if
it is merely said, parāmarśājanyam anumitih, the defect of ativyāpti
occurs in parāmarsadhvamsa, as it is also parāmarśajanya. To avoid
this, the term jñānam is inserted in the definition.] Indubious
knowledge of the thing to be proved is the counter-acting factor
in inferential knowledge (ie when the thing to be proved is
ascertained in the paksa, the need to infer its existence is
superfluous). Even when the thing to be proved is perceived,
sometimes it is observed that a desire: I shall infer, stimulates
one to take to inference (to establish the proven). Here,
the factor that stimulates one (to take to inference) is the
desire to establish a thing in the paksa. Just as the absence
of a pratibandhaka mani (the gem capable of neutralising
the burning power of fire) in association with the absence
of a uttejaka mani (a gem that stimulates the power of burning
in fire) is the cause of heat, even so, the absence of indubious
knowledge of the thing to be proved in association with the
absence of a desire to predicate a thing in the paksa is
the cause of inferential knowledge. 
[88. When a particular variety of gem capable of neutralising the
burning power of fire is placed near fire, the gem obstructs the
burning power of fire; but, if some stimulator gem is placed near
the counter-acting agent, it effectively neutralises the latter. Here,
the absence of a desire to establish a thing — sişādhayişāvirahatva
is given as a delimiting adjunct — viśeşana to indubious knowledge
— siddhi, and not to the absence of indubious knowledge. The absence
of stimulating-gem is given as a delimiting adjunct to the obstructor-gem.
When the obstructor-gem is present in association with the
stimulator-gem, there will be the power of burning in fire even like
power of burning remaining intact when there is the absence of
obstructor-gem in association with the absence of stimulator-gem.
Similarly, of the two, viz, (1) indubious knowledge, and, (2) a desire
to predicate a thing in the paksa, the former is the obstructing
agent — pratibandhaka, while the latter is the stimulator agent — uttejaka.
Inferential knowledge is brought about consequent on the absence
of indubious knowledge in association with the absence of a desire
to establish or predicate a thing]

verses.original_text:
3. Subsumptive reflection (parāmarśa):
व्याप्तिविशिष्टपक्षधर्मताज्ञानं परामर्शः । यथा वह्निव्याप्यधूमवानयं
पर्वत इति ज्ञानं परामर्शः । तज्जन्यं पर्वतो वह्निमानिति
ज्ञानमनुमितिः ॥ ३ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
3. Subsumptive reflection is the knowledge of reason
existing in the subject accompanied by invariable
concomitance (of sādhya), eg the knowledge: this
mountain has smoke which is invariably pervaded by fire
is parāmarsa; and the cognition that results from it
and takes the form: the mountain is fiery is the inferential
knowledge.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
परामर्श लक्षयति व्याप्तीति । व्याप्तिविषयकं यत्पक्षधर्मताज्ञानं स परामर्श
इत्यर्थः । परामर्शमभिनीय दर्शयति यथेति । अनुमितिमभिनीय दर्शयति
तज्जन्यमिति । परामर्शजन्यमित्यर्थः । व्याप्तिलक्षणमाह यत्रेति ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The Author defines parāmarśa: vyāpti.... The meaning is
that the knowledge of the existence of reason in an instance
which has invariable concomitance as its object, is subsumptive
reflection. The Author explains parāmarśa by declaration through
the words: Yathā.... Similarly, he explains inferential knowledge
through the usage of words, tajjanyam.... The meaning is:
inferential knowledge is born of subsumptive reflection.

verses.original_text:
4. Invariable concomitance - vyāpti:
यत्र यत्र धूमस्तत्र तत्राग्निरिति साहचर्यनियमो व्याप्तिः ॥ ४ ॥

commentaries.author: English Paraphrase
commentaries.commentary:
4. Invariable concomitance is the certainty of
co-existence like, wherever there is smoke there is fire.

commentaries.author: Sanskrit Commentary
commentaries.commentary:
यत्र धूमस्तत्राग्निः इति व्याप्तेरभिनयः । साहचर्यनियमः इति लक्षणम् । साहचर्य
सामानाधिकरण्यं, तस्य नियमः । हेतुसमानाधिकरणात्यन्ताभावाप्रतियोगिसाध्यसामाना-
धिकरण्यं व्याप्तिरित्यर्थः ।

commentaries.author: English Translation
commentaries.commentary:
Dīpikā:
The characteristic mark of vyāpti is given in the Text:
yatra.... The Text further illustrates vyāpti through the words:
where there is smoke there is also fire. The distinguishing
characteristic mark of invariable concomitance is the invariability
of concomitance. Concomitance is co-existence (of smoke and
fire) in one and the same substratum; its certainty is niyama.
The fuller definition of vyāpti is: the quality of being co-existent
in the same substratum with the thing to be proved which
does not become the counter-entity of its own absolute negation
that co-exists with reason in the same substratum. 
[89. Take for example, smoke and fire. The said vyapti becomes
applicable to smoke. What could be the negation that co-exists in
the mountain with the reason, smoke? It can be the negation of
pot, but not the negation of fire, because where there is smoke,
there is also fire. Fire thus cannot become the counter-entity of
its negation. With that fire (which does not become the counter-entity
